nativeRange.habitat: Steep outer reef drop-offs, rubble areas, and among growths of black coral and sea fans; typically found at depths of 22–80 m

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented consuming worms, bottom-dwelling invertebrates, and plankton. In captivity, generally accepts finely chopped crustacean flesh and mysis shrimp; may require coaxing initially.

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: D. Terver, Muséum-Aquarium de Nancy. Non-commercial use with attribution.

summary: The Mitratus Butterflyfish is a striking deep-reef species from the Western Indian Ocean, recognized by its yellow body with two diagonal black stripes and a distinctive eye mask. Generally considered intermediate in care and best kept in a large, well-decorated aquarium as a mated pair.
careNotes: The Mitratus Butterflyfish, also sold under the names Indian Butterflyfish and Black and Yellow Butterflyfish, is a deepwater species naturally found at 22–80 m depth along steep outer reef drop-offs, among growths of black coral and sea fans. It tends to be peaceful toward other fish of similar temperament, though it is generally considered unsuitable for reef aquariums, as it has been known to nip at corals and ornamental invertebrates. Small groups of up to five individuals have been observed in the wild; in captivity it is generally advised to keep the species singly or as a mated pair. The Mitratus Butterflyfish has been documented forming monogamous pair bonds, and purchasing an established pair is often recommended. Feeding can require some patience — finely chopped crustacean flesh, mysis shrimp, and varied frozen foods are all worth offering. A large aquarium with ample live rock and open swimming space is recommended, with a minimum tank size of around 500 liters.
breedingNotes: The Mitratus Butterflyfish is an egg-laying (oviparous) species that forms monogamous pair bonds in the wild. Captive breeding has not been reliably documented for this species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (14.0 cm SL vs LiveAquaria 5.5 in/14 cm — consistent), native range, habitat depth, temperature (24.4–26.9°C vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C — minor spread, averaged to 23–26.5°C), taxonomy, and grouping behavior. Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; BlueZoo and Fishkeeping World had no species page — FishBase used as sole confirmed secondary.

fish.breedingNotes: The species is egg-laying (oviparous) and forms monogamous pair bonds in the wild. No reliable captive breeding records have been established.
